title: Fitting secondary glazing to a bay window
subtitle: Measuring, cutting, and fitting an acrylic secondary-glazing kit to a three-light bay
excerpt: A single-glazed bay window can account for 30-40 percent of the heat loss in a Victorian front room. Secondary glazing on the inside reduces the U-value from around 5.0 to 2.5 W/m2K without altering the external appearance.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust secondary glazing guidance for U-value comparisons. AECB Low-Energy Building Database for measured secondary-glazing performance in traditional buildings. Historic England advice on glazing in listed and conservation-area properties.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of heat flow through a building element, in watts per square metre per degree Kelvin (W/m2K). Lower is better: a U-value of 0.16 loses half as much heat as one of 0.32.

content.text: A Victorian bay window with three single-glazed sash lights has a combined area of around 3-4 m2. At a single-glazing

of 5.0 W/m2K and a design temperature difference of 20 degrees C, that window loses around 300-400 W continuously during a cold winter day. Secondary glazing on the room side reduces the U-value to 2.5 W/m2K, halving the heat loss. The noise reduction is a further benefit: secondary glazing typically reduces traffic noise by 40-45 dB when the inner pane is correctly sealed.

Secondary glazing on the inside face of a window does not alter the external appearance and does not require planning permission in most cases. Check with your local authority if the property is listed or in a conservation area with specific conditions on internal alterations.
A three-light bay has a central section facing directly forward and two angled side sections. Measure each section separately. The measurements needed for each pane are:
Width: measure at the top, middle, and bottom of the opening. Use the smallest measurement, reduced by 10 mm per side to allow the secondary panel to seat in its channel without binding.
Height: measure from the inner window board to the top of the opening at three points. Use the smallest measurement, reduced by 5 mm at the top.

Angle of the side returns: a typical bay is set at 45 degrees to the front face. Measure this with a digital angle finder or a protractor placed against the corner. The secondary panel for a side return is not rectangular; the side edge is cut at the bay angle.

The standard DIY approach uses 4 mm acrylic sheet cut to size. The sheet sits in a plastic magnetic channel fixed to the window reveal. Acrylic is lighter than glass and cuts with a utility knife. The magnetic channel lets you lift the panel out in summer for ventilation. A cheaper option is a low-e film applied to the existing glass, but it is less effective: it cuts the U-value by around 0.5 W/m2K rather than 2.5 W/m2K.

Check the window reveal for level and square. Use the spirit level on the vertical sides of the reveal. Mark any significant deviation: a reveal that is more than 5 mm out of plumb over 1 m height needs packing under the channel to keep the acrylic panel hanging true.

Fix the magnetic channel sections to the reveal with the supplied screws. Start with the top channel, then the two sides, then the sill piece. Drill pilot holes in the reveal before screwing to avoid cracking plaster.

Cut the acrylic panel to size. Score the acrylic three to four times along a straight edge with the utility knife, then snap it over the edge of a table. Clean up the cut edge with fine wet-and-dry abrasive paper.
Press the magnetic strip along all four edges of the acrylic panel. The strip clips onto the edge of the acrylic and the magnet on the strip mates with the metal insert in the channel on the reveal.
Offer up the panel and press it into the channels. Check the seal around the perimeter by pressing your hand along each edge on a windy day. Any gap produces a detectable cold draught.

Secondary glazing creates a sealed air gap between the inner panel and the existing window. In cold weather, moisture can condense on the inside face of the existing (outer) window within this gap if the gap seal is very tight. This is most common in rooms with high humidity (living rooms with gas fires, kitchens). A small piece of silica gel desiccant placed in the air gap absorbs moisture and prevents persistent condensation. Refresh the silica gel by placing it in a warm oven for an hour once per season.

attrs.symptom: Acrylic panel distorts and bows inward
attrs.cause: Panel was cut too large and is under compression in the channel
attrs.fix: Remove the panel and trim 2-3 mm from the edges that are binding. Acrylic expands at around 0.07 mm per metre per degree C; leave 5 mm clearance per metre of panel dimension in the channels.

attrs.symptom: Panel drops out of the channel in cold weather
attrs.cause: Acrylic contracted, reducing the contact with the magnetic strip
attrs.fix: Fit secondary magnetic channel strips along the bottom edge rather than relying on gravity to hold the panel. Alternatively, use a clip-on channel that mechanically retains the panel rather than a magnetic system.
